riskImpact: Without explicit Cache-Control, browsers and intermediary proxies may cache sensitive pages — exposing authenticated content to subsequent users on shared devices or to cached responses on a CDN.
explanation: All responses should have an explicit Cache-Control directive appropriate to the content type.
fixSteps: For sensitive/authenticated pages: Cache-Control: no-store
For static assets: Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable
For dynamic pages: Cache-Control: no-cache, must-revalidate

codeExamples.label: Next.js — sensitive pages

title: Missing Network Error Logging (NEL) header
description: The Network Error Logging header is absent. NEL enables automatic reporting of network-level failures to a reporting endpoint.
evidence: NEL header is absent from the response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without NEL, network-level failures (DNS errors, connection timeouts, TLS errors) go undetected until users report them manually. This delays identifying CDN, DNS, or certificate issues affecting users.
explanation: NEL instructs browsers to collect and report network failures to a specified endpoint.
fixSteps: Add a Report-To header with a reporting endpoint
Add the NEL header referencing that endpoint group
Ensure your reporting endpoint can handle NEL reports

codeExamples.label: Next.js

title: Missing Document-Policy header
description: The Document-Policy header is not present. Document-Policy can restrict feature usage and require specific behaviors within a document.
evidence: Document-Policy header is absent from the response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without Document-Policy, there is no mechanism to enforce document-level restrictions like disabling synchronous XHR, requiring JS profiling opt-in, or limiting oversized images — all of which affect performance and security posture.
explanation: Document-Policy enforces behaviors at the document level, complementing Permissions-Policy.
fixSteps: Identify which Document-Policy features apply to your use case
Add Document-Policy header with the desired directives
Test that restricted features behave as expected

codeExamples.label: Next.js — disable sync XHR

title: Missing Origin-Agent-Cluster header
description: The Origin-Agent-Cluster header is not set, meaning the site shares an agent cluster with same-site cross-origin pages.
evidence: Origin-Agent-Cluster header is absent from the response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without origin-keyed agent clusters, same-site cross-origin pages can share memory and synchronous access via document.domain — weakening isolation and Spectre mitigations.
explanation: Origin-Agent-Cluster: ?1 requests an origin-keyed agent cluster, providing stronger isolation from same-site origins.
fixSteps: Add Origin-Agent-Cluster: ?1 to all responses
Ensure you do not rely on document.domain for cross-origin communication (use postMessage)

codeExamples.label: Next.js

title: Missing Report-To header
description: No Report-To header is configured, so browser security policy violations are not being collected.
evidence: Report-To header is absent from the response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without a reporting endpoint, CSP violations, COOP violations, and network errors go unreported. Violations are a signal of attempted attacks — without them, attacks go completely undetected.
explanation: The Report-To header defines where browsers send violation reports for CSP, COOP, and NEL.
fixSteps: Implement a reporting endpoint at /api/reports or use a service like report-uri.com
Add the Report-To header with the endpoint URL
Reference the group name in CSP report-to directive

codeExamples.label: Next.js

title: NEL header not configured
description: Network Error Logging is not configured via the NEL header, preventing automatic collection of network-level failures.
evidence: NEL header is absent from the response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Network failures like DNS resolution errors, connection timeouts, and TLS handshake failures that affect real users remain invisible without NEL reporting.
explanation: NEL collects client-side network failure events and reports them to your specified endpoint.
fixSteps: Configure a Report-To header with a Nel reporting group
Add NEL header referencing that group
Monitor reports for patterns indicating infrastructure issues

codeExamples.label: NEL setup

title: Overly broad Access-Control-Expose-Headers
description: The Access-Control-Expose-Headers response header exposes a broad set of response headers to cross-origin JavaScript.
evidence: Broad Access-Control-Expose-Headers detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Exposing response headers like Set-Cookie, Authorization, or internal tracking headers to cross-origin JavaScript leaks information that was intended to be opaque to the requesting page.
explanation: Only expose the headers that cross-origin clients actually need to read.
fixSteps: Review which headers cross-origin clients need to access
List only those specific headers in Access-Control-Expose-Headers
Never expose Set-Cookie, Authorization, or internal headers

codeExamples.label: Expose only necessary headers

title: Access-Control-Expose-Headers: * exposes all headers
description: Access-Control-Expose-Headers is set to a wildcard, exposing all response headers to cross-origin JavaScript.
evidence: Access-Control-Expose-Headers: * detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Exposing all headers means any cross-origin page that has CORS access can read every response header — including internal routing headers, server version info, and any sensitive metadata that was not intended to be public.
explanation: Only list the specific headers that cross-origin clients need to access.
fixSteps: Replace * with an explicit list of required headers
Never include headers like Set-Cookie, Authorization, or internal headers in the list

codeExamples.label: Explicit header list
codeExamples.code:
headers["Access-Control-Expose-Headers"] = "Content-Length, X-Total-Count";
// Not: Access-Control-Expose-Headers: *

title: CORS preflight cache time is too long
description: The Access-Control-Max-Age value is very large, caching CORS preflight results for an excessively long time.
evidence: Access-Control-Max-Age with excessive value detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: A very long preflight cache means browsers will not re-check CORS permissions if you change your policy — potentially allowing stale permissions to persist for days or weeks on the client.
explanation: Set Access-Control-Max-Age to a reasonable value (600 seconds recommended) to balance performance and policy update latency.
fixSteps: Set Access-Control-Max-Age to 600 seconds (10 minutes) or less
Browsers impose their own cap (Chrome: 7200s, Firefox: 86400s)

codeExamples.label: Reasonable preflight cache

title: CSP missing upgrade-insecure-requests directive
description: The Content-Security-Policy header does not include upgrade-insecure-requests, leaving HTTP sub-resources unprotected.
evidence: CSP without upgrade-insecure-requests detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without upgrade-insecure-requests, HTTP URLs for images, scripts, and other resources are not automatically upgraded to HTTPS — enabling mixed content attacks that downgrade connections.
explanation: The upgrade-insecure-requests directive instructs browsers to treat all HTTP resource requests as HTTPS.
fixSteps: Add upgrade-insecure-requests to your CSP header
Verify your CSP does not also include block-all-mixed-content (pick one)

codeExamples.label: Add to existing CSP

title: CSP allows data: URIs in script or object sources
description: The Content-Security-Policy permits data: URIs as a script or object source, enabling data-URI-based script execution.
evidence: CSP with data: in script-src or object-src detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Allowing data: in script-src allows attackers to execute base64-encoded scripts via XSS — this is functionally similar to allowing unsafe-inline and bypasses many CSP protections.
explanation: data: URIs in script-src are treated as unsafe by CSP Level 3. Remove data: from any security-critical directive.
fixSteps: Remove data: from script-src and object-src
Limit data: to img-src or font-src where needed for inline images or fonts
Use nonces for any inline scripts that were previously base64-encoded data URIs

codeExamples.label: Safe data: usage

title: Permissions-Policy grants excessive browser feature access
description: The Permissions-Policy header allows broad access to sensitive browser features like camera, geolocation, or microphone for all origins.
evidence: Overly permissive Permissions-Policy detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Granting access to sensitive features to all origins means any third-party iframe embedded on the page can request those permissions from the user — without the user understanding it is the iframe, not your site, making the request.
explanation: Follow least-privilege: only allow the specific origins that need each feature.
fixSteps: Audit which features your application actually uses
Disable features you do not use: feature=()
Limit features you do use to your own origin: feature=(self)

codeExamples.label: Restrictive Permissions-Policy

title: Deprecated Feature-Policy header in use
description: The Feature-Policy header is present. This header has been replaced by Permissions-Policy.
evidence: Feature-Policy header detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Feature-Policy is no longer supported in modern browsers. Relying on it means your feature restrictions may be silently ignored, leaving the page without the intended browser feature controls.
explanation: Feature-Policy was renamed to Permissions-Policy in 2020. Use Permissions-Policy instead.
fixSteps: Replace the Feature-Policy header with Permissions-Policy
Update the directive syntax (Permissions-Policy uses a different format)
Remove the Feature-Policy header entirely once Permissions-Policy is set

codeExamples.label: Migrate to Permissions-Policy

title: X-Content-Type-Options set to an invalid value
description: The X-Content-Type-Options header is present but set to a value other than "nosniff", making it ineffective.
evidence: X-Content-Type-Options with invalid value detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An incorrect value like X-Content-Type-Options: true or X-Content-Type-Options: 1 is ignored by browsers — leaving MIME sniffing enabled and the page vulnerable to content-type confusion attacks.
explanation: The only valid value for X-Content-Type-Options is "nosniff" (lowercase).
fixSteps: Set X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (the only valid value)
Remove any other value you may have set

codeExamples.label: Correct value

title: HSTS header missing preload directive
description: The Strict-Transport-Security header is present but does not include the preload directive, preventing inclusion in browser HSTS preload lists.
evidence: HSTS header without preload directive detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without the preload directive and inclusion in the HSTS preload list, first-time visitors who type the domain without https:// may connect over HTTP before being redirected — exposing that first request to interception.
explanation: The preload directive enables submission to browser preload lists so the domain is always loaded over HTTPS, even on the first visit.
fixSteps: Add preload to your HSTS header: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload
Ensure max-age is at least 31536000 and includeSubDomains is present
Submit to the preload list at hstspreload.org

codeExamples.label: Next.js
Nginx
codeExamples.code: add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload" always;

codeExamples.label: Apache
codeExamples.code: Header always set Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload"

title: Server header discloses software identity
description: The HTTP Server response header identifies the web server software by name.
evidence: Server header with software name detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Knowing the server software (nginx, Apache, IIS, etc.) enables targeted vulnerability scanning against known CVEs for that software and version, reducing the effort needed for an attack.
explanation: Remove or genericize the Server header to reduce information leakage.
fixSteps: Nginx: add "server_tokens off;" in the http block
Apache: set "ServerTokens Prod" and "ServerSignature Off"
Next.js: set poweredByHeader: false in next.config.mjs

codeExamples.label: Nginx
codeExamples.code:
# In nginx.conf http block:
server_tokens off;
# This removes the version; to hide the server name entirely:
# server_tokens "VulnRadar";  # or any generic string

codeExamples.label: Apache
codeExamples.code:
ServerTokens Prod
ServerSignature Off

title: Server header reveals detailed version number
description: The Server header exposes the exact version of the web server software, enabling targeted version-specific attacks.
evidence: Detailed server version string detected in Server header at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exact version like "nginx/1.18.0" allows an attacker to instantly identify applicable CVEs and patch status — removing the need to probe for vulnerabilities manually.
explanation: Set Server to a product-only token without version, or remove it entirely.
fixSteps: Nginx: server_tokens off removes the version number
Apache: ServerTokens Prod shows only "Apache" without version
Consider removing the header entirely via middleware

codeExamples.label: Nginx — hide version

title: X-Powered-By header reveals application framework
description: The X-Powered-By response header discloses the application framework and sometimes version (e.g., "Express", "PHP/8.1").
evidence: X-Powered-By header detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Knowing the framework allows attackers to focus on framework-specific vulnerabilities, default paths, and known misconfigurations — reducing reconnaissance effort significantly.
explanation: Remove the X-Powered-By header.
fixSteps: Next.js: poweredByHeader: false in next.config.mjs
Express: app.disable("x-powered-by")
PHP: expose_php = Off in php.ini

codeExamples.label: Next.js
codeExamples.code:
// next.config.mjs
export default { poweredByHeader: false };

codeExamples.label: Express
codeExamples.code:
app.disable("x-powered-by");
// Or use helmet: app.use(helmet({ hidePoweredBy: true }));

title: X-AspNet-Version header exposes ASP.NET version
description: The X-AspNet-Version header is present and reveals the exact ASP.NET framework version.
evidence: X-AspNet-Version header detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: The specific ASP.NET version allows attackers to immediately identify applicable CVEs and security patches that have not been applied, narrowing exploit selection.
explanation: Disable the X-AspNet-Version header in your application configuration.
fixSteps: In Web.config: <httpRuntime enableVersionHeader="false" />
Or via custom header removal in Global.asax

codeExamples.label: Web.config
codeExamples.code:
<configuration>
  <system.web>
    <httpRuntime enableVersionHeader="false" />
  </system.web>
</configuration>

title: X-AspNetMvc-Version header exposes MVC version
description: The X-AspNetMvc-Version header reveals which version of ASP.NET MVC the application is running.
evidence: X-AspNetMvc-Version header detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: The specific MVC version helps attackers identify framework-specific vulnerabilities and apply targeted exploits.
explanation: Disable the header in your MvcHandler configuration.
fixSteps: In Global.asax Application_Start: MvcHandler.DisableMvcResponseHeader = true;

codeExamples.label: Global.asax.cs

title: Via header reveals proxy infrastructure
description: The Via header is present and reveals proxy server software names or versions in the request chain.
evidence: Via header with infrastructure details detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: The Via header can reveal internal proxy names, software versions, or infrastructure topology — giving attackers a map of the request chain and potential pivot points.
explanation: Strip or genericize the Via header at your edge proxy.
fixSteps: Configure your CDN/proxy to remove or genericize the Via header
Nginx: proxy_set_header Via "";
Ensure internal hostnames are not reflected in Via headers

codeExamples.label: Nginx — remove Via

title: X-Runtime header reveals request processing time
description: The X-Runtime header is present, exposing per-request server processing time in milliseconds.
evidence: X-Runtime header detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Request timing information can be used for timing-based side-channel attacks — inferring whether a user exists, whether a resource is cached, or the cost of specific operations.
explanation: Remove the X-Runtime header in production.
fixSteps: Disable X-Runtime in your framework configuration
Rails: config.action_dispatch.x_runtime_header = nil

codeExamples.label: Nginx — remove runtime header
codeExamples.code: proxy_hide_header X-Runtime;

title: X-Request-Id header exposed to clients
description: The X-Request-Id header is returned in the response and exposes internal request tracking identifiers.
evidence: X-Request-Id header detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Internal request IDs may leak details about infrastructure, help attackers correlate requests across systems, or reveal predictable ID schemes that enable enumeration or replay attacks.
explanation: Request IDs used for internal logging can be stripped from client-facing responses.
fixSteps: Remove X-Request-Id from client responses while keeping it in internal logs
If needed for debugging, only return it to authenticated users

codeExamples.label: Strip in Nginx
codeExamples.code: proxy_hide_header X-Request-Id;

title: X-Backend-Server header reveals backend hostname
description: The X-Backend-Server header discloses the internal hostname or IP of the backend server that handled the request.
evidence: X-Backend-Server header with internal hostname detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Internal hostnames and IPs reveal your infrastructure topology, making it easier to target specific backend servers, bypass load balancers, or enumerate internal services.
explanation: Remove all headers that expose backend server identity.
fixSteps: Configure your load balancer or CDN to strip X-Backend-Server
Audit all custom headers set by backend services for internal information

codeExamples.label: Nginx — strip backend headers

title: Age header reveals CDN cache age
description: The Age header is present and reveals how long the response has been cached at a CDN or proxy.
evidence: Age header detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Age headers reveal CDN infrastructure is in use and indicate whether a response is served from cache — helping attackers time cache-poisoning attacks or infer when cached entries will expire.
explanation: Age is a standard HTTP header; consider removing it for sensitive responses if it reveals operational details.
fixSteps: For sensitive responses, set Cache-Control: no-store (CDN will not cache them, so Age will not appear)
For public resources, Age is expected behavior and low risk

codeExamples.label: Remove Age for sensitive endpoints

title: Debug header present in production response
description: A debug-related response header is present (e.g., X-Debug-Info, X-Debug-Token, X-Symfony-Debug), revealing application internals.
evidence: Debug response header detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Debug headers expose internal application state, configuration, database query counts, or profiler data — information that helps attackers understand the application and identify inefficiencies to target.
explanation: Debug headers must not be present in production responses.
fixSteps: Disable debug mode in production
Audit your framework config to ensure debug output is suppressed
Symfony: remove the Debug toolbar; Laravel: set APP_DEBUG=false

codeExamples.label: Strip debug headers in Nginx

title: X-Amz-Request-Id reveals AWS infrastructure
description: The X-Amz-Request-Id header is present, revealing that the response is served from AWS infrastructure.
evidence: X-Amz-Request-Id header detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: While low severity on its own, revealing AWS infrastructure enables attackers to tailor attacks to AWS-specific vulnerabilities, targeting S3 public bucket access, EC2 metadata endpoints, or IAM misconfigurations.
explanation: Strip AWS-specific headers at your edge proxy before they reach clients.
fixSteps: Configure your CDN or reverse proxy to strip X-Amz-* headers
Ensure S3 bucket responses are proxied and not directly accessed

codeExamples.label: Nginx — strip AWS headers

title: Cloudflare CF-Ray request identifier present
description: The Cloudflare CF-Ray response header exposes the request identifier that Cloudflare assigns to every proxied request.
evidence: Cloudflare CF-Ray header present - reveals CDN usage.
riskImpact: CF-Ray alone does not leak sensitive information, but it confirms your origin sits behind Cloudflare and lets attackers correlate request IDs to logs.
explanation: CF-Ray is informational — it lets support teams trace a specific request through Cloudflare's edge. It does not contain user data, PII, or session tokens.
fixSteps: CF-Ray cannot be removed from Cloudflare responses. If you want to hide CDN usage, terminate the proxy at the origin (no Cloudflare).

If you want to keep Cloudflare but reduce fingerprinting, ensure all other Cloudflare-specific headers (cf-ray, cf-cache-status, cf-worker) are also present so attackers cannot easily distinguish them.

codeExamples.label: Cloudflare Transform Rule
codeExamples.code:
# Disable if you migrate off Cloudflare. Otherwise leave it for traceability.
# In Cloudflare dashboard → Rules → Transform Rules → Modify Response Header
# Action: Remove Header → Name: cf-ray

title: X-Vercel-Id reveals deployment platform
description: The X-Vercel-Id header is present and reveals the application is hosted on Vercel.
evidence: X-Vercel-Id header detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Revealing the deployment platform enables targeted attacks specific to Vercel's architecture, including serverless function cold-start timing attacks, deployment URL enumeration, or platform-specific configuration issues.
explanation: Vercel headers can be suppressed via custom Next.js middleware.
fixSteps: In Next.js middleware, you can strip headers before they are forwarded

codeExamples.label: Next.js middleware — strip Vercel headers

title: X-Cache header reveals CDN cache status
description: The X-Cache header (e.g., X-Cache: HIT or MISS) is present and reveals CDN caching details.
evidence: X-Cache header detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Cache HIT/MISS status can be used to probe CDN behavior, aid cache poisoning attacks by identifying when poison entries are cached, and reveal which resources are cached by CDN nodes.
explanation: X-Cache is an informational header; strip it for sensitive responses.
fixSteps: Configure your CDN to not forward X-Cache to end users
Most CDNs have a header suppression option in their dashboard

codeExamples.label: Nginx
codeExamples.code:
proxy_hide_header X-Cache;
proxy_hide_header X-Cache-Hit;

title: ETag reveals server inode number
description: The ETag header format suggests it includes a file inode number, potentially revealing internal server file system details.
evidence: ETag with potential inode information detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: inode-based ETags leak the file's inode number on the server, which can be used to identify server software, configuration, and in some cases to correlate requests across virtual hosts sharing the same filesystem.
explanation: Apache's default ETag format includes inode, file size, and modification time. Configure it to exclude the inode.
fixSteps: Apache: FileETag MTime Size (removes inode)
Nginx generates ETags from last-modified time and content length — no inode included by default

codeExamples.label: Apache — remove inode from ETag
codeExamples.code:
# In httpd.conf or .htaccess:
FileETag MTime Size
# The default "FileETag All" includes inode — this removes it

title: ETag value may contain inode information
description: The ETag response header contains a value that appears to embed inode numbers from the server filesystem.
evidence: ETag with inode-style format detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Exposing inode numbers helps attackers fingerprint the exact web server software version and may reveal filesystem structure that aids in targeted path traversal or LFI attacks.
explanation: Change Apache's ETag configuration to exclude inode values.
fixSteps: Apache: "FileETag MTime Size" in httpd.conf or .htaccess removes inode from ETags
Alternatively, disable ETags entirely: "FileETag None"

codeExamples.label: Apache — disable inode in ETags
codeExamples.code:
FileETag MTime Size
# Or disable ETags entirely if not needed:
# FileETag None
# Header unset ETag

title: Server-Timing header exposes internal metrics
description: The Server-Timing response header reveals internal performance metrics such as database query times, cache status, or backend service timings.
evidence: Server-Timing header with detailed metrics detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Detailed server timing data reveals internal architecture (database, cache, rendering steps), helps attackers identify slow operations for timing attacks, and provides a map of backend services being called.
explanation: Restrict Server-Timing to non-sensitive metric names and only expose it in development or to internal monitoring.
fixSteps: In production, disable Server-Timing or use opaque metric names
Gate Server-Timing behind an internal IP check or admin auth
Add Timing-Allow-Origin to restrict which origins can read the timings

codeExamples.label: Conditional Server-Timing

title: Server Date header shows clock skew
description: The Date response header shows a time significantly different from the current time, indicating a misconfigured server clock.
evidence: Server Date header with clock skew detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Severe clock skew can cause authentication token expiration issues, TOTP/HOTP MFA code rejection, TLS certificate validation failures, and session management problems. Predictable skew can also help attackers infer server restart times or virtualization artifacts.
explanation: Ensure NTP synchronization is configured and running on all servers.
fixSteps: Enable and configure NTP: systemctl enable --now systemd-timesyncd
Verify time sync: timedatectl status
For containers, ensure the host clock is synchronized and the container inherits it

codeExamples.label: Linux — enable NTP sync
codeExamples.code:
# Enable systemd-timesyncd
systemctl enable --now systemd-timesyncd

# Check sync status
timedatectl show --property=NTPSynchronized

# Or install and configure chrony for better accuracy:
apt-get install chrony
service chrony restart

title: X-DNS-Prefetch-Control: off disables browser DNS prefetch
description: X-DNS-Prefetch-Control is set to "off", disabling browser DNS prefetching for links on the page.
evidence: X-DNS-Prefetch-Control: off detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Disabling DNS prefetch does not improve security but reduces performance — the browser will not pre-resolve DNS for links on the page. This increases navigation latency for users. This header is sometimes set as a misguided "security" measure.
explanation: X-DNS-Prefetch-Control: off is often added under the mistaken belief it improves privacy. Consider removing it or setting it to "on" for better performance.
fixSteps: Remove the X-DNS-Prefetch-Control: off header
If privacy is the concern, add <link rel="dns-prefetch"> only to explicit trusted origins

codeExamples.label: Remove the header or set to on

title: Cache-Control: public on sensitive resource
description: A response containing authenticated or user-specific content is served with Cache-Control: public, allowing it to be cached by shared proxies.
evidence: Cache-Control: public detected on authenticated endpoint at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Caching authenticated responses at shared proxy caches (CDNs, corporate proxies) means one user's private data may be served to another user — a critical data leakage vulnerability.
explanation: Authenticated and user-specific responses must use Cache-Control: private or no-store to prevent shared caching.
fixSteps: Change Cache-Control to "no-store" for authenticated/private responses
Use "private, no-cache" for responses that vary per user but can be re-validated
Review your CDN configuration to ensure it does not cache authenticated routes

codeExamples.label: Correct for authenticated responses

title: Page can be embedded in a frame (clickjacking risk)
description: The page does not set X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors, making it embeddable in an iframe by any site.
evidence: No frame protection headers detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without frame protection, an attacker can embed your page in a transparent iframe over their own page — tricking users into clicking buttons or confirming actions on your site without their knowledge (clickjacking).
explanation: Set X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors to control which origins can embed your page.
fixSteps: Add X-Frame-Options: DENY to block all framing
Or use CSP: frame-ancestors 'none' (more flexible)
If your site embeds itself, use SAMEORIGIN or frame-ancestors 'self'

codeExamples.label: Next.js

title: Deprecated TLS version (TLS 1.0 or 1.1) supported
description: The server supports TLS 1.0 or TLS 1.1, which are deprecated protocols with known security weaknesses.
evidence: Deprecated TLS version supported at {{url}}.
riskImpact: TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are vulnerable to BEAST, POODLE, and other downgrade attacks that can allow an attacker to decrypt the connection — exposing credentials, session tokens, and all transmitted data.
explanation: Only TLS 1.2 and 1.3 should be accepted. TLS 1.0 and 1.1 were deprecated by RFC 8996 in 2021.
fixSteps: Configure your web server to only accept TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3
Disable TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 in your server configuration
Prefer TLS 1.3 which provides forward secrecy by default

codeExamples.label: Nginx — TLS 1.2+ only

title: HTTP resources loaded on HTTPS page (mixed content)
description: An HTTPS page loads scripts, images, or other resources over HTTP, triggering browser mixed content warnings.
evidence: Mixed content (HTTP resources on HTTPS page) detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Active mixed content (scripts, iframes, forms) is blocked by modern browsers. Passive mixed content (images) is loaded but can be intercepted by a network attacker — replacing images, injecting watermarks, or fingerprinting users via the unencrypted HTTP request.
explanation: All resources must be loaded over HTTPS when the page is served over HTTPS.
fixSteps: Update all resource URLs from http:// to https://
Add upgrade-insecure-requests to your CSP to auto-upgrade HTTP requests
Use protocol-relative URLs (//example.com) or absolute HTTPS URLs

codeExamples.label: CSP upgrade-insecure-requests

title: Form submits data over unencrypted HTTP
description: An HTML form uses an HTTP (not HTTPS) action URL, transmitting form data in plaintext.
evidence: Form with HTTP action URL detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Form data submitted over HTTP is visible to any network observer — exposing passwords, personal data, payment information, or authentication tokens to interception.
explanation: All form actions must use HTTPS URLs.
fixSteps: Change the form action from http:// to https://
Enable HSTS to prevent HTTP downgrades
Audit all forms to verify action URLs use HTTPS

codeExamples.label: Correct form action
codeExamples.code:
<!-- BAD: <form action="http://example.com/submit"> -->
<!-- GOOD: -->
<form action="https://example.com/submit" method="POST">
  <!-- Always use HTTPS for form submissions -->
</form>

title: Form on HTTPS page submits to HTTP endpoint
description: A form on an HTTPS page has an HTTP action URL, causing the form data to be submitted unencrypted.
evidence: Form with HTTP action URL on HTTPS page at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Even though the page itself is served over HTTPS, form data submitted to an HTTP endpoint travels unencrypted — exposing credentials, payment data, or personal information to interception by a network attacker.
explanation: Form action URLs must be HTTPS when the page is served over HTTPS.
fixSteps: Update all form action URLs to use https://
Ensure redirect chains from HTTP form actions also use HTTPS

codeExamples.label: Correct form action
codeExamples.code:
<form action="https://yoursite.com/submit" method="POST">
  <input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="current-password" />
  <button type="submit">Log in</button>
</form>

title: External script loaded without Subresource Integrity
description: A script or stylesheet is loaded from an external origin without a Subresource Integrity (SRI) hash.
evidence: External resource without SRI integrity attribute at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without SRI, if the CDN or third-party host serving the resource is compromised, attackers can replace the file with malicious code that executes in every visitor's browser — a supply chain attack.
explanation: SRI allows browsers to verify that a fetched resource has not been tampered with by checking a cryptographic hash.
fixSteps: Generate hash: openssl dgst -sha384 -binary file.js | openssl base64 -A
Add integrity="sha384-HASH" and crossorigin="anonymous" to the element
Lock CDN URLs to specific versioned filenames

codeExamples.label: Script with SRI
codeExamples.code:
<script
  src="https://cdn.example.com/lib.min.js"
  integrity="sha384-COMPUTED_HASH"
  crossorigin="anonymous"
></script>

title: External stylesheet without SRI
description: A CSS stylesheet loaded from a third-party CDN does not have a Subresource Integrity hash.
evidence: External stylesheet without SRI detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: A compromised CDN stylesheet can inject CSS-based data exfiltration techniques, CSS keyloggers, or modify the visual appearance of the page to enable phishing — without any JavaScript.
explanation: SRI must be applied to all external CSS resources.
fixSteps: Generate hash: openssl dgst -sha384 -binary style.css | openssl base64 -A
Add integrity and crossorigin to the link element

codeExamples.label: Stylesheet with SRI
codeExamples.code:
<link
  rel="stylesheet"
  href="https://cdn.example.com/styles.min.css"
  integrity="sha384-COMPUTED_HASH"
  crossorigin="anonymous"
/>

title: Third-party script tag missing SRI attribute
description: An externally hosted script is included in the page without an integrity attribute.
evidence: External script without SRI integrity attribute at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without SRI, compromise of the external script host results in immediate code execution in all visitors' browsers. Third-party CDNs are high-value targets precisely because they serve content to millions of sites simultaneously.
explanation: Add SRI to every external script tag. Generate hashes using openssl or SRI hash generator tools.
fixSteps: Add integrity="sha384-HASH" to the script tag
Add crossorigin="anonymous"
Consider self-hosting critical scripts to eliminate CDN dependency

codeExamples.label: Script with SRI
codeExamples.code:
<script
  src="https://cdn.example.com/analytics.min.js"
  integrity="sha384-HASH_HERE"
  crossorigin="anonymous"
  async
></script>

title: External linked stylesheet without integrity hash
description: A link element referencing an external stylesheet does not include an SRI integrity attribute.
evidence: External link stylesheet without SRI at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Stylesheets without SRI can be replaced by a compromised CDN with malicious CSS that exfiltrates form field values, adds phishing content, or performs attribute-based keylogging — all without any JavaScript.
explanation: Generate and add an SRI hash to all external stylesheet links.
fixSteps: Compute: openssl dgst -sha384 -binary file.css | openssl base64 -A
Add integrity and crossorigin attributes to the link tag

codeExamples.label: Link with SRI
codeExamples.code: <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.example.com/lib.min.css" integrity="sha384-HASH" crossorigin="anonymous" />

title: Cookies missing security attributes
description: One or more cookies are set without required security attributes (Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite).
evidence: Cookies without security attributes detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Cookies without Secure can be sent over HTTP (interceptable). Without HttpOnly, scripts can steal session cookies via XSS. Without SameSite, cookies are sent on cross-site requests, enabling CSRF attacks.
explanation: All session and authentication cookies must have Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite attributes set.
fixSteps: Add Secure to prevent sending over HTTP
Add HttpOnly to prevent JavaScript access
Add SameSite=Strict or Lax to prevent CSRF

codeExamples.label: Secure cookie attributes

title: No clickjacking protection headers
description: Neither X-Frame-Options nor CSP frame-ancestors is set, leaving the page vulnerable to clickjacking attacks.
evidence: No clickjacking protection detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without frame protection, an attacker can embed your page in a transparent iframe on their malicious site, overlaying UI elements to trick users into unknowingly clicking buttons — approving transfers, changing settings, or confirming actions on your site.
explanation: Set X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors to prevent unauthorized framing.
fixSteps: Add X-Frame-Options: DENY to all responses
Or use CSP frame-ancestors: frame-ancestors 'none' (more powerful)
Test that your own legitimate iframes still work if you use SAMEORIGIN

codeExamples.label: Next.js

title: Frame-busting relies on header only without JS fallback
description: Clickjacking protection is provided by HTTP header only, without a JavaScript frame-busting script for older browsers.
evidence: Frame protection limited to HTTP header without JS fallback at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Older browsers that do not support X-Frame-Options may be tricked into framing the page. While modern browsers all support X-Frame-Options, the JS fallback provides defense in depth for legacy clients.
explanation: Combine HTTP header protection with a JavaScript frame-busting snippet for legacy browser coverage.
fixSteps: Keep X-Frame-Options header
Add a JS frame-buster for legacy browsers
Consider CSP frame-ancestors which supersedes X-Frame-Options in modern browsers

codeExamples.label: JS frame buster

title: CORS allows wildcard origin with credentials
description: The server returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * alongside Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, which is invalid and exploitable.
evidence: CORS wildcard with credentials header combination detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: While browsers block this specific combination per spec, a misconfigured CORS policy that echoes back the Origin header with Allow-Credentials: true means any malicious site can make authenticated cross-origin requests with the victim's cookies — reading private API data.
explanation: Wildcard origins and credentials cannot be used together. Use an explicit origin allowlist with credentials.
fixSteps: Never use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with Allow-Credentials: true
Validate the Origin header against an allowlist and echo back the specific allowed origin
Set Vary: Origin to prevent caching issues

codeExamples.label: Correct CORS with credentials

title: Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true with wildcard origin
description: Access-Control-Allow-Credentials is set to true on a response that uses a wildcard or dynamic origin without proper validation.
evidence: Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true with wildcard or unvalidated origin at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An attacker can host a malicious page that makes authenticated cross-origin requests to your API using the victim's session cookies — reading private account data, performing state-changing actions, or exfiltrating sensitive information.
explanation: When Allow-Credentials is true, the allowed origin must be a specific trusted origin, never *.
fixSteps: Validate the request Origin against an explicit allowlist
Only set Allow-Credentials: true for validated trusted origins
Never reflect an arbitrary Origin header without validation

codeExamples.label: Safe credentials CORS

title: CORS allows overly broad HTTP methods
description: Access-Control-Allow-Methods lists methods (e.g., PUT, DELETE, PATCH) that the endpoint does not actually use.
evidence: Overly permissive CORS methods detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Granting CORS access to methods beyond what is needed enables cross-origin requests to trigger state changes (PUT, DELETE) from malicious sites — combining with CSRF for authenticated destructive actions.
explanation: List only the HTTP methods that cross-origin clients actually need to use.
fixSteps: Audit which methods each endpoint supports
Only list those specific methods in Access-Control-Allow-Methods
Return 405 for methods not in your allowlist

codeExamples.label: Minimal CORS methods

title: Access-Control-Allow-Methods set to wildcard
description: Access-Control-Allow-Methods is set to *, permitting any HTTP method in cross-origin requests.
evidence: Access-Control-Allow-Methods: * detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: A wildcard methods policy means any cross-origin request using any HTTP verb is permitted — including DELETE, PUT, PATCH — enabling cross-origin destructive operations against your API.
explanation: Always specify an explicit list of allowed methods in CORS preflight responses.
fixSteps: Replace * with the specific methods your endpoint supports
Typical APIs need: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE — not all six verbs

codeExamples.label: Explicit methods
codeExamples.code:
headers["Access-Control-Allow-Methods"] = "GET, POST, PUT";
// Not: Access-Control-Allow-Methods: *

title: No rate limiting detected on API endpoint
description: The API endpoint does not appear to enforce rate limiting, allowing unlimited requests.
evidence: No rate-limiting headers (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After) detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without rate limiting, an attacker can make unlimited requests to brute-force passwords or OTPs, enumerate user accounts, trigger expensive server operations, or perform DoS attacks against your infrastructure.
explanation: Implement rate limiting on all API endpoints, with stricter limits on authentication and sensitive operations.
fixSteps: Use a middleware like express-rate-limit or Upstash Rate Limit for Next.js
Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After when limit is exceeded
Apply stricter limits to /login, /signup, /forgot-password

codeExamples.label: Next.js with Upstash Rate Limit

title: Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy (COEP) header not set
description: The COEP header is absent, preventing the page from achieving cross-origin isolation.
evidence: COEP header missing from response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without COEP, the page cannot enable cross-origin isolation — blocking use of SharedArrayBuffer, high-resolution timers, and other isolation-dependent APIs, while leaving the page exposed to Spectre-class cross-process attacks.
explanation: COEP: require-corp forces all loaded subresources to opt in to cross-origin sharing, enabling cross-origin isolation.
fixSteps: Add Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp
Also add Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin for full isolation
Verify all embedded resources provide CORP headers or CORS

codeExamples.label: Full isolation headers

title: COOP report-only mode not configured
description: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy is not set in report-only mode for testing before full enforcement.
evidence: COOP-Report-Only header absent at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without testing COOP in report-only mode, you may break legitimate popup or OAuth flows when you enforce COOP. Using report-only first lets you catch these issues before they affect users.
explanation: Use Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy-Report-Only to test COOP before enforcing it.
fixSteps: Add Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy-Report-Only: same-origin
Monitor violation reports for popup/OAuth breakage
Switch to Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy once violations are resolved

codeExamples.label: COOP report-only

title: Sec-Fetch-* headers not validated server-side
description: The application does not appear to validate Sec-Fetch-* request headers to verify request context.
evidence: Sec-Fetch headers not enforced at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without Sec-Fetch validation, the server cannot distinguish same-origin form submissions from cross-site requests, navigate-type requests from XHR, or user-initiated requests from automated ones — making CSRF mitigation harder.
explanation: Sec-Fetch-* headers are set by browsers and provide verifiable request context. Validate them for additional CSRF protection.
fixSteps: Check Sec-Fetch-Site for cross-site requests on state-changing endpoints
Reject requests where Sec-Fetch-Mode is not appropriate for the endpoint

codeExamples.label: Validate Sec-Fetch-Site

title: Referrer-Policy not set to strict-origin-when-cross-origin
description: The Referrer-Policy header is absent or not set to a strict value, potentially leaking full URL referrers to cross-origin destinations.
evidence: Referrer-Policy not set to a strict value at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without a strict Referrer-Policy, the full URL of pages (including query strings with tokens, user IDs, or search terms) is sent in the Referer header to every third-party origin — leaking user activity and potentially sensitive URL parameters.
explanation: strict-origin-when-cross-origin is the recommended value — it sends the full URL for same-origin requests but only the origin for cross-origin HTTPS requests.
fixSteps: Add Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin to all responses

codeExamples.label: Next.js

title: HSTS header missing includeSubDomains directive
description: The Strict-Transport-Security header is present but does not include includeSubDomains, leaving subdomains unprotected.
evidence: HSTS without includeSubDomains detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without includeSubDomains, attackers can downgrade connections to subdomains over HTTP and set cookies (without Secure flag) that are sent to the main domain — a subdomain cookie injection attack.
explanation: includeSubDomains extends HSTS protection to all subdomains of the current domain.
fixSteps: Add includeSubDomains to the HSTS header
Ensure all subdomains are served over HTTPS before adding includeSubDomains
Consider also adding preload for first-visit protection

codeExamples.label: Next.js
Nginx
codeExamples.code: add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload" always;

codeExamples.label: Apache
codeExamples.code: Header always set Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload"

title: Password field lacks visibility toggle
description: The password input field does not have a show/hide toggle, making it harder for users to verify what they typed.
evidence: Password field without visibility toggle detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without a toggle, users cannot verify their password entry, leading to more authentication failures and frustration. Users may write passwords down or choose simpler passwords to avoid typos — both weaken security.
explanation: A password visibility toggle improves usability without compromising security when implemented correctly.
fixSteps: Add a toggle button that switches type between "password" and "text"
Use an eye/eye-off icon as the toggle button
Default to hidden (type="password"); the user opts in to seeing it

codeExamples.label: Password toggle

title: Email input missing autocomplete attribute
description: The email input field does not specify autocomplete="email", preventing browsers and password managers from autofilling it.
evidence: Email input without autocomplete attribute detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without autocomplete="email", password managers cannot reliably fill in the email field, reducing usability and potentially discouraging use of password managers — which correlates with weaker credential habits.
explanation: Set autocomplete="email" on email input fields to enable browser and password manager autofill.
fixSteps: Add autocomplete="email" to all email input fields

codeExamples.label: Email input with autocomplete
codeExamples.code: <input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email" required />

title: Credit card input missing autocomplete attributes
description: Credit card input fields do not use standardized autocomplete values, preventing password managers and browser autofill from working.
evidence: Credit card inputs without autocomplete detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without proper autocomplete values, users must type card numbers manually — increasing errors and reducing checkout completion. It also makes the page incompatible with payment-focused password managers like 1Password.
explanation: Use standardized autocomplete values for payment fields.
fixSteps: Card number: autocomplete="cc-number"
Expiry: autocomplete="cc-exp"
CVV: autocomplete="cc-csc" (note: many password managers do not store CVV)

codeExamples.label: Payment form autocomplete
codeExamples.code:
<input type="text" name="cc-number" autocomplete="cc-number" inputmode="numeric" />
<input type="text" name="cc-exp" autocomplete="cc-exp" />
<input type="text" name="cc-csc" autocomplete="cc-csc" inputmode="numeric" />

title: Search input missing type="search"
description: A search input field uses type="text" instead of type="search", missing browser-native search UI features.
evidence: Search input without type="search" detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Minimal security impact, but missing type="search" means the browser does not show a clear button, does not style the field as a search box, and search queries are not distinguished from other text inputs in browser history.
explanation: Use type="search" for search fields to get native browser features and semantic correctness.
fixSteps: Change type="text" to type="search" on search input fields
Add role="search" to the containing form for accessibility

codeExamples.label: Correct search input
codeExamples.code:
<form role="search">
  <input type="search" name="q" placeholder="Search..." autocomplete="off" />
  <button type="submit">Search</button>
</form>

title: Telephone input missing autocomplete attribute
description: A tel input field does not have autocomplete="tel", preventing browser and password manager autofill.
evidence: Telephone input without autocomplete detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without autocomplete="tel", users must type their phone number manually each time, reducing form completion rates and discouraging use of the form.
explanation: Add autocomplete="tel" or a specific sub-type to enable autofill.
fixSteps: Add autocomplete="tel" to telephone input fields

codeExamples.label: Tel input with autocomplete
codeExamples.code: <input type="tel" name="phone" autocomplete="tel" placeholder="+1 (555) 000-0000" />

title: Image element missing alt attribute
description: An img element is missing the alt attribute, making it inaccessible to screen readers.
evidence: img element without alt attribute detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Images without alt text are announced as the filename by screen readers, creating a confusing experience for visually impaired users. This is an accessibility violation (WCAG 1.1.1) and may cause legal exposure in jurisdictions with accessibility requirements.
explanation: All images must have an alt attribute — either descriptive text or an empty string for decorative images.
fixSteps: Add alt="Description of image" for informational images
Add alt="" for purely decorative images (screen readers will skip them)
Never use alt="image" or alt="photo" — describe what the image shows

codeExamples.label: Correct alt usage

title: External link missing rel="noopener noreferrer"
description: A link targeting a new tab or external origin does not include rel="noopener noreferrer", exposing the page to reverse tabnapping.
evidence: External link without rel="noopener noreferrer" at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without noopener, the opened page has access to window.opener and can redirect the original tab to a phishing page without the user noticing — known as reverse tabnapping.
explanation: Add rel="noopener noreferrer" to all links that use target="_blank" or point to external origins.
fixSteps: Add rel="noopener noreferrer" to all target="_blank" links
Modern browsers have partially addressed this, but adding the attribute is still best practice

codeExamples.label: Safe external link
codeExamples.code: External site

title: Form submits to a non-HTTPS URL
description: A form action URL does not use HTTPS, transmitting data in cleartext.
evidence: Form with non-HTTPS action at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Data submitted through the form travels unencrypted and can be intercepted by a network observer — exposing passwords, payment data, or personal information.
explanation: All form actions must use HTTPS.
fixSteps: Update the form action to use https://
Ensure the destination server has a valid TLS certificate
Enable HSTS on the destination to prevent protocol downgrades

codeExamples.label: Secure form action
codeExamples.code:
<form action="https://yoursite.com/submit" method="POST">
  <input type="hidden" name="_csrf" value="{{ csrfToken }}" />
  <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>

title: Meta refresh redirect with missing or empty URL
description: A meta refresh tag is present but the URL attribute is missing or empty, causing unpredictable redirect behavior.
evidence: Meta refresh without URL detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: A meta refresh without a URL reloads the current page in a loop, causing poor user experience. If the URL can be injected by user input, it becomes an open redirect vulnerability.
explanation: Meta refresh should always point to an explicit absolute HTTPS URL, or better — be replaced with a server-side redirect.
fixSteps: Replace meta refresh with a server-side 301/302 redirect
If meta refresh must be used, set an explicit absolute HTTPS URL
Never derive the meta refresh URL from user input

codeExamples.label: Server-side redirect (preferred)

title: Video iframe missing allowfullscreen attribute
description: An iframe embedding video content (e.g., YouTube) does not include the allowfullscreen attribute.
evidence: Video iframe without allowfullscreen at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Minimal security impact. Without allowfullscreen, users cannot expand the embedded video to fullscreen — degrading the user experience for video content.
explanation: Add allowfullscreen to video iframes where fullscreen viewing is expected.
fixSteps: Add allowfullscreen attribute to video iframes
Also add sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin" to restrict other iframe capabilities

codeExamples.label: Video iframe with allowfullscreen
codeExamples.code:
<iframe
  src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/VIDEO_ID"
  allowfullscreen
  loading="lazy"
  title="Product demo"
  sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin allow-presentation"
></iframe>

title: Iframe missing loading="lazy" attribute
description: Offscreen iframes do not use the loading="lazy" attribute, causing them to load eagerly and slow page load.
evidence: Iframe without loading="lazy" at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Minimal security impact. Eagerly loading offscreen iframes increases page load time and wastes bandwidth on content the user may never see — hurting Core Web Vitals.
explanation: Add loading="lazy" to iframes that are below the fold.
fixSteps: Add loading="lazy" to all iframes not needed for initial viewport render

codeExamples.label: Lazy iframe
codeExamples.code:
<iframe
  src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/VIDEO_ID"
  loading="lazy"
  title="Video"
></iframe>

title: Username field missing autocomplete="username"
description: A username or login field does not specify autocomplete="username", hindering password manager autofill.
evidence: Username input without autocomplete attribute detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without autocomplete="username", password managers cannot reliably identify the username field — reducing autofill accuracy and discouraging password manager use.
explanation: Set autocomplete="username" on username and login identifier fields.
fixSteps: Add autocomplete="username" to the username/login field
Pair with autocomplete="current-password" on the password field

codeExamples.label: Login form with autocomplete
codeExamples.code:
<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="username" />
<input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="current-password" />

title: Image URL uses protocol-relative scheme (//)
description: An image source uses a protocol-relative URL (//example.com/image.jpg) that inherits the page protocol.
evidence: Protocol-relative image URL detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: If the page is served over HTTP (e.g., after a redirect failure or HSTS bypass), protocol-relative image URLs also load over HTTP — enabling mixed content and potential image replacement by a network attacker.
explanation: Use explicit https:// URLs for all external resources.
fixSteps: Replace // with https:// in all image src attributes
This also ensures images load correctly if the HTML is shared or stored outside a web server context

codeExamples.label: Explicit HTTPS image

title: OpenGraph image URL is not HTTPS
description: The og:image meta tag uses an HTTP URL, which may cause browsers and social platforms to block the image.
evidence: og:image with HTTP URL detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: HTTP image URLs in OG tags are blocked by major social platforms (Twitter/X, LinkedIn, Facebook) and browsers on HTTPS pages — causing missing preview images when the URL is shared. An attacker on the network can also replace the HTTP image with their own content.
explanation: All OpenGraph image URLs must use HTTPS.
fixSteps: Change og:image content from http:// to https://
Ensure the image server has a valid TLS certificate

codeExamples.label: HTTPS og:image
codeExamples.code: <meta property="og:image" content="https://yoursite.com/preview.jpg" />

title: Canonical link element is missing
description: The page does not have a <link rel="canonical"> element specifying the preferred URL.
evidence: Canonical link element absent at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without a canonical URL, search engines may index duplicate content versions (www vs non-www, HTTP vs HTTPS, with vs without trailing slash) — splitting page authority and causing SEO issues. This also makes it easier to serve phishing pages at alternate URLs without detection.
explanation: Set a canonical link to the definitive HTTPS URL of each page.
fixSteps: Add <link rel="canonical" href="https://yoursite.com/page"> to the page head
Use the same URL pattern consistently across all pages

codeExamples.label: Canonical link
codeExamples.code: <link rel="canonical" href="https://yoursite.com/current-page" />

title: Viewport meta tag is missing
description: The page does not include a viewport meta tag, causing incorrect rendering on mobile devices.
evidence: Viewport meta tag absent at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without a viewport tag, mobile browsers render the page at desktop width and scale it down — making UI elements tiny and hard to interact with. This worsens the user experience and indirectly reduces security if users cannot read HTTPS indicators or warning dialogs clearly.
explanation: The viewport meta tag controls how the page is displayed on mobile devices.
fixSteps: Add <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1"> to the <head>

codeExamples.label: Viewport meta
codeExamples.code: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />

title: HTML DOCTYPE declaration is missing
description: The page does not start with a DOCTYPE declaration, triggering quirks mode in some browsers.
evidence: DOCTYPE declaration absent at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without a DOCTYPE, browsers may render the page in quirks mode — a legacy compatibility mode with different CSS box model behavior, inconsistent XSS filtering (in old browsers), and unpredictable rendering that can break security UI elements.
explanation: Every HTML page must start with <!DOCTYPE html>.
fixSteps: Add <!DOCTYPE html> as the very first line of every HTML document

codeExamples.label: Correct DOCTYPE
codeExamples.code:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8" />
  <title>Page Title</title>
</head>

title: Excessive inline style attributes
description: The page contains 3 or more HTML elements with inline style attributes, which CSP cannot inspect and which complicate theming.
evidence: ${count} elements have inline style= attributes (CSP hygiene).
riskImpact: Move inline styles to external stylesheets or design tokens.
explanation: Inline style attributes cannot be validated against Content-Security-Policy rules like `style-src 'self'` and they couple layout to markup. External stylesheets or CSS modules make the page easier to theme and audit.
fixSteps: Move recurring inline styles into the global CSS or component-level CSS modules.
For dynamic styles (e.g. inline transform values), prefer CSS variables and `style={{ '--var': value }}` over raw inline rules.
Set a CSP `style-src` directive that excludes 'unsafe-inline' once inline styles are eliminated.

codeExamples.label: React with CSS variables
codeExamples.code:
<div
  className="card"
  style={{ '--card-width': `${width}px` } as React.CSSProperties}
>

codeExamples.label: Nginx CSP
codeExamples.code: add_header Content-Security-Policy "style-src 'self'" always;

title: Reverse Tabnabbing — target=_blank without rel=noopener
description: Anchor tags use target="_blank" without rel="noopener", allowing the opened page to call window.opener.location = '...' and redirect the source page to a phishing site.
evidence: ${count} target="_blank" link(s) lack rel="noopener" (reverse tabnabbing).
riskImpact: Add rel="noopener noreferrer" to every link with target="_blank". Modern browsers (Chrome 88+, Firefox 79+) default rel="noopener" for target="_blank", but explicit is still recommended for older browsers.
explanation: When a link opens a new tab with target='_blank' and no rel='noopener', the new page receives a reference to window.opener and can navigate the source page to any URL. This is the classic reverse-tabnabbing phishing vector.
fixSteps: Add rel="noopener noreferrer" to all <a> tags with target="_blank".
In Next.js / React, use <Link target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer"> for external links.
Audit third-party embed scripts (analytics, support widgets) that open links in new tabs.

codeExamples.label: Next.js / React
codeExamples.code: Star us on GitHub

codeExamples.label: Static HTML
codeExamples.code: Visit

title: Bare email address in HTML vulnerable to spam harvesting
description: An email address is present in the HTML source as plain text or a mailto: link, making it harvestable by spam bots.
evidence: Email address in HTML source detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Email addresses in HTML source are harvested by spam bots within minutes of being indexed. This exposes the address owner to massive amounts of spam, phishing attempts, and social engineering.
explanation: Protect email addresses from harvesting using obfuscation, contact forms, or server-side delivery.
fixSteps: Use a contact form instead of a bare email address
If a mailto: link is required, load it via JavaScript from an API endpoint
Consider Cloudflare Email Obfuscation or similar service

title: Third-party iframe without sandbox attribute
description: An iframe embedding third-party content does not use the sandbox attribute, giving the embedded content full access to the parent page context.
evidence: Third-party iframe without sandbox detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: A third-party iframe without sandbox can access parent DOM via same-origin JavaScript, open new windows, send forms, and potentially escalate privileges if the third-party is compromised — a supply-chain attack vector.
explanation: Add sandbox to all third-party iframes and grant only the permissions actually needed.
fixSteps: Add sandbox="" (most restrictive)
Add allow-scripts if the iframe requires JavaScript
Never combine allow-scripts with allow-same-origin for cross-origin content

codeExamples.label: Sandboxed third-party iframe
codeExamples.code:
<iframe
  src="https://widget.third-party.com"
  sandbox="allow-scripts"
  loading="lazy"
  title="Widget"
></iframe>
